content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Lindsay Beavers 
Deputy County Counsel 
Office of the Fresno County Counsel 
2220 Tulare Street, Suite 500 
Fresno, CA 93 721 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-028 
Dear Ms. Beavers: 
August 12, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Fresno County regarding the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") and Government Code 
Section 1090, et seq. 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under these provisions, and not 
under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest, 
including Public Contract Code. · 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (197 5) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assm:nes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Fresno County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We received a written 
response from the District Attorney stating that office has an interest in the subject of this request, 
and thus refrains from comment. We did not receive a written response from the Attorney General's 
Office. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 
1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other 
than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1 ( c )( 5).) 
QUESTION 
Under the Act or Section 1090, may the County enter contracts with the employers of 
individuals who serve on the Local Justice Assistance Grant Program ("JAG") Steering Committee 
using JAG grant funds, where the Local Steering Committee's role is advisory, and the State 
requires the Committee represent a significant cross-section of juvenile and/or criminal justice 
stakeholders? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSION 
File No. A-19-028 
Page No. 2 
Yes. To the extent that the Local JAG Steering Committee does not meet the criteria for 
having decision-making authority under Regulation 18700( c )(2), individuals who serve on the 
committee are not public officials under the Act and are not prohibited from seeking a grant 
awarded by the County. Where the State requires that the advisory body, the Local Steering 
Committee, be comprised of stakeholders who may have financial interests in the award of grant 
funds, the narrow exception to Section 1090 articulated by the Attorney General, and discussed 
below, is applicable. (82 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 126.) 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTOR 
Background 
The Edward Byrne Memorial Justice Assistance Grant ("JAG") Program is administered by 
the federal Office of Justice Programs ("OJP") and authorized under 34 U.S.C. §§ 10151 - 10158. 
The JAG Program provides states, tribes, and local governments with funding to support a range of 
law enforcement-related programs. It is the "leading federal source of criminal justice funding to 
state and local jurisdictions." 2 Federal law requires that a grant request from a state or local 
government provide a "comprehensive plan" designed in consultation with "all representatives of 
all s~gments of the criminal justice system, including judges, prosecutors, law enforcement 
personnel, corrections personnel, and providers of indigent defense services, victim services, 
juvenile justice delinquency prevention programs, community corrections, and reentry services." 
(34 U.S.C. §10153(A)(6).) 
The State's JAG Program grants are distributed by the Board of State and Community 
Corrections ("BSCC"). The Fresno County Probation Department is currently in the· application 
process for a BSCC JAG grant, in response to the BSCC's Request for Proposals ("RFP") released 
in December 2018. At the state level, the BSCC uses an Executive Steering Committee to make 
decisions related to-the JAG Program. The JAG Executive Steering Committee is composed of 
subject matter experts and stakeholders representing both the public and the private sectors. The 
JAG Executive Steering Committee submits grant award recommendations to the BSCC, which 
may approve, reject, or revise those recommendations. The JAG Program RFP explicitly states that 
existing law prohibits anyone who participated on the JAG Executive Steering Committee from 
receiving funds from the grant applicants who are awarded funds. 3 
2 Funds may be used for law enforcement programs, prosecution, ·indigent defense, courts, crime prevention 
and education, corrections and community corrections, drug treatment and enforcement, planning, evaluation, 
technology improvement, and crime victim and witness initiatives and mental health programs and related law 
enforcement and corrections programs, including behavioral programs and crisis intervention teams. See 
https://www.bja.gov/jag/index.html, as viewed March 19, 2019. 
3 The BSCC's JAG RFP states: 
Existing law prohibits any non-governmental sub-grantee, partner or like party who participated 
on this JAG ESC from receiving funds from the JAG awarded under this RFP. Applicants who are 
awarded grants under this RFP are responsible for reviewing the JAG ESC membership roster and 


Local JAG Steering Committee 
File No. A-19-028 
Page No. 3 
To apply for the BSCC JAG funding, the County must form a Local JAG Steering 
Committee comprised of local stakeholders who have expertise in the prospective interventions to 
be addressed by the JAG proposal. The Local Steering Committee determines the community needs 
and develops a three-year JAG strategy in one-year increments, in accordance with the state's 
strategy priorities. The Local JAG Steering Committee is required to "represent a significant cross-
section of juvenile and/or criminal justice stakeholders" within the County, and must include a 
balanced representation of both traditional and non-traditional stakeholders. Examples of non-
traditional stakeholders could include community-based and faith-based organizations, educators, 
social service providers, family member of a criminal justice involved person, job developers, 
advocacy groups, or citizens. Examples of traditional stakeholders could include law enforcement, 
district attorney, probation, courts, and other city and county departments.4 The County determines 
the total number of members to serve on the Local JAG Steering Committee. The County currently 
has representatives from county agencies such as the District Attorney's Office, Public Defender's 
Office, and the Sheriffs Office, as "traditional stakeholders" on the Local Steering Committee. It 
also has several local nonprofit organizations on the Committee. 
The BSCC's RFP states that applicants must "consider state and local conflict of interest 
laws" when selecting members of their Local Steering Committee.5 It does not state that existing 
law prohibits Local JAG Steering Committee members from receiving grant funds. 
You state that the Local Steering Committee "does not have decision-making authority." 
You describe its role as to "merely identify community needs and priorities for grant funds, like 
lack of housing, lack of education" and to determine the intervention type to fill that need, like GED 
classes, low-barrier temporary housing, and to develop strategies and projects to address those 
needs. This role includes developing written operational policies and procedures for the Local 
Steering Committee and providing ongoing oversight for the project. The County then works to 
ensuring that no grant dollars are passed through to any non-governmental entity represented by 
the members of the JAG ESC. (Appendix A). 
Applicants should also consider state and local conflict of interest laws when selecting members 
of their Local JAG Steering Committee. The Applicant is advised to check with its counsel about 
potential conflicts. (BSCC JAG RFP, 2019-2022 for Counties, released December 6, 2018, page 
3.) 
4 The BSCC JAG RFP states the following requirements: 
Stakeholders identified for membership on the Local JAG Steering Committee shall possess a 
working knowledge of the problem areas being discussed within the identified JAG priorities. 
The Local JAG Steering Committee will work collaboratively to identify the needs of the 
community as they relate to the JAG priorities and to create and develop a comprehensive project 
plan with the overall goal ofreducing violent crime and recidivism within their county. 
(BSCC JAG RFP, 2019-2022 for Counties, ibid, page 10.) 
5 See fn. 5. 


FileNo. A-19-028 
Page No. 4 
procure services to fill those needs through its own County procurement process, without decision-
making input from the Local JAG Steering Committee. 
You provided additional information by email regarding the Local JAG Steering 
Committee's role in the process. Specifically, the Committee will prepare recommendations and a 
project plan to identify and fulfill the community needs, which will be sent to the County Board of . 
Supervisors. The Committee will not make recommendations regarding the local agencies who may 
provide the services it recommends. The County Board of Supervisors has the discretion to change 
or amend the project plan prior to issuing its approval. If the County is awarded the grant, it will go 
through the County's purchasing process or a formal RFP to select the vendors. The Committee will 
meet 2-3 times in a 3-4-month period before the grant is awarded, and then will meet once every 6 
months to a year to oversee the project and make sure the grant funds are being properly utilized. In 
its oversight role, the Local JAG Steering Committee will be providing general oversight·of the 
grant objectives, ensuring the objectives are being met, and major goals are being accomplished. 
The County, as the awarding body of the individual service contracts, will be providing oversight of 
the individual contracts for services. If these services are not being adequately provided by a 
particular contractor, the County will terminate a contract with any breaching contractor. 
This is the first time that the County's Probation Department has applied for a JAG grant. 
Regarding past County applications for a JAG grant, you did not find a record of local steering 
committees being formed, or required, under those grant applications. In response to our inquiry 
regarding the County's history in approving advisory committee recommendations, County staff 
reports that the County Board of Supervisors does not have a practice of "rubber-stamping" 
recommendations, and that the County Board of Supervisors reviews every item that comes before 
it, and approves each item based on the information proviped by staff, after a careful weighing of 
the item's risks and benefits. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. Section 82048 defines a "public 
official" as "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency." 
The threshold inquiry is whether the individual members serving on the Local JAG Steering 
Committee meet this definition. 
Regulation 18700( c )(2) specifically excludes from the definition of "member" those 
individuals who serve on committees that lack decision-making authority. A committee has 
decision-making authority when: 
(i) It may make a final governmental decision; 


File No. A-19-028 
Page No. 5 
(ii) It may compel or prevent a governmental decision either by reason of 
an exclusive power to initiate the decision or by reason of a veto that may not be 
overridden; or 
(iii) It makes substantive recommendations and, over an extended period 
oftime, those recommendations have been regularly approved without significant 
amendment or modification by another public official or governmental agency. 
(Regulation 18700(c)(2)(A).) 
Based on the facts provided, the Local Steering Committee does not appear to have 
decision-making authority. Therefore, the individuals serving on the Local JAG Steering 
Committee are excluded from the definition of "public official" under the Act and the individuals 
would not be subject to the Act in seeking a grant awarded by the County. 
Section 1090 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officers from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at 
actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." (City of Imperial Beach v. 
Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
The prohibition of Section 1090 is not restricted to the specific officer or board that "makes" 
the contract. The prohibition covers consultants and advisers who are involved in the preliminary 
planning for the contract, including discussions, negotiations, reasoning, or the drawing of plans 
and specifications for the contract. (See e.g., Stigall, supra, at 569-571; City Council v. McKinley 
(1978) 80 Cal.App.3d 204.) When a contract is to be executed by the governing body of a public 
entity and a member of the body has a proscribed interest, the prohibition cannot be avoided by 
having the interested member abstain; the entire governing body is precluded from entering into the 
contract. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 649-650; Stigall, supra, at 569.) 
This requirement has been applied differently in regard to advisory boards or committees. 
For an advisory board, which may have i_nfluence over the contracting decisions, but does not 
execute the contract, the entire board is not disqualified so long as the interested member abstains 
from any type of participation, including giving advice, related to the decision. (82 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 126 (1999).) · 
Such abstention is not possible, however, where an advisory board is statutorily required to 
contain financially interested persons and the Attorney General has found that the conflict of 
interest is deemed allowable by the Legislature. In the 1999 opinion noted above, the Attorney 
General stated that applying the general prohibition of Section 1090 in such circumstances or to 
require the financially interested board members to abstain would undermine the Legislature's 


File No. A-19-028 
Page No. 6 
express determination that interested persons render advice, as members of the advisory board. (82 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 126, supra, at 130.) 
In 2016, we applied this exception where a state grant program required the county to form 
an advisory multidisciplinary team, and an advisory steering committee to recommended services 
for the grant funds. (Page Advice Letter, No. A-16-037.) The enabling statute required the county 
"multidisciplinary team" to include persons trained in child abuse or neglect issues, and qualified to 
provide a broad range of related services. The county was required to document its collaboration 
with partner agencies and children-focused organizations. The county steering committee's make-
up followed the state's suggested template, and included representatives of agencies and 
organizations with financial interests related to these types of children's services. The advice 
focused on the state's purpose in including financially interested members on the advisory bodies, 
and stated: 
It is apparent that these agencies, individuals and private organizations all play 
important roles and provide essential services for CSEC [Commercially Exploited 
Children]. This is the very reason they were singled out for membership on the 
[multidisciplinary team]. In many cases their roles and services are unique. For 
example, only the Juvenile Court, (which is one of the listed agencies), 
adjudicates matters relating to CSEC. To deny funds to the Juvenile Court or 
other agencies or private entities identified in the MOU would be inconsistent 
with the Legislature's intent in funding these types of activities. We do not 
believe that the Legislature contemplated such a result. Accordingly, _SSA [ county 
social services agency] is not prohibited from entering into Proposed Contracts 
with the agencies and private.entities that are represented on the MDT. 
And addressing the steering committee: 
As is the case with the [multidisciplinary team], the advisory role of the Steering 
Committee includes making recommendations for needed services. The MOU 
expressly lists agencies and organizations that may serve on the Steering 
Committee. These agencies and organizations all play important roles and provide 
essential services for CSEC. This is the very reason they were singled out for 
membership on the [multidisciplinary team]. We believe that by including a 
requirement for a steering committee in the Template and approving the MOU 
that contains the requirement, the state contemplated that agencies and 
organizations serving on the Steering Committee because of their expertise would 
be permitted to enter into the subject contracts. Otherwise, the Legislature's 
purpose in adopting a program for funding the needs of CSEC might be thwarted. 
Therefore, we find that under the exception, employers of the individuals serving 
on the Steering Committee may enter into the contracts with SSA to provide 
CSEC services. 
In this matter, the State's JAG Program and the BSCC's RFP requires that that the Local 
Steering Committee be comprised of "a significant cross-section of juvenile and/or criminal justice 
stakeholders within.the applicant county," and tasked with identifying priorities and intervention 
types for the grant. As noted in the language from the RFP, the state specifically identifies the 


File No. A-19-028 
Page No. 7 
traditional stakeholders ( e.g., law enforcement, district attorney) and the nontraditional stakeholders 
( e.g., community and faith-based organizations and social service providers) as the types of 
representation needed on the steering committee. (See fn.5) 
Recognizing the potential for conflict at the state executive steering committee level, the 
BSCC specifically excludes anyone who participated on the JAG Executive Steering Committee 
from receiving funds from the grant applicants who are awarded funds. (See fn. 7.) Notably, the 
BSCC does not state that the conflict of interest laws prohibits the local steering committee 
members from receiving grant funds. As stated in the .2016 advice letter, it is difficult to see how a 
local steering committee required to include local stakeholders could be formed absent potentially 
financially interested members. Therefore, we advise that the narrow exception articulated in 82 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 126 (1999) for advisory boards where the make-up of members is required by 
the program to include financially interested persons, is appli.cable to this matter. The County is not 
prohibited under Section 1090 from entering into contracts with the employer or business of a Local 
JAG Steering Committee member. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
LKH:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
Under the Act or Section 1090, may the County enter contracts with the employers of 
individuals who serve on the Local Justice Assistance Grant Program ("JAG") Steering Committee 
using JAG grant funds, where the Local Steering Committee's role is advisory, and the State 
requires the Committee represent a significant cross-section of juvenile and/or criminal justice 
stakeholders?

funds, the narrow exception to Section 1090 articulated by the Attorney General, and discussed 
below, is applicable. (82 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 126.)

sections.analysis:
The Act 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. Section 82048 defines a "public 
official" as "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency." 
The threshold inquiry is whether the individual members serving on the Local JAG Steering 
Committee meet this definition. 
Regulation 18700( c )(2) specifically excludes from the definition of "member" those 
individuals who serve on committees that lack decision-making authority. A committee has 
decision-making authority when: 
(i) It may make a final governmental decision;  
(ii) It may compel or prevent a governmental decision either by reason of 
an exclusive power to initiate the decision or by reason of a veto that may not be 
overridden; or 
(iii) It makes substantive recommendations and, over an extended period 
oftime, those recommendations have been regularly approved without significant 
amendment or modification by another public official or governmental agency. 
(Regulation 18700(c)(2)(A).) 
Based on the facts provided, the Local Steering Committee does not appear to have 
decision-making authority. Therefore, the individuals serving on the Local JAG Steering 
Committee are excluded from the definition of "public official" under the Act and the individuals 
would not be subject to the Act in seeking a grant awarded by the County. 
Section 1090 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officers from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at 
actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." (City of Imperial Beach v. 
Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
The prohibition of Section 1090 is not restricted to the specific officer or board that "makes" 
the contract. The prohibition covers consultants and advisers who are involved in the preliminary 
planning for the contract, including discussions, negotiations, reasoning, or the drawing of plans 
and specifications for the contract. (See e.g., Stigall, supra, at 569-571; City Council v. McKinley 
(1978) 80 Cal.App.3d 204.) When a contract is to be executed by the governing body of a public 
entity and a member of the body has a proscribed interest, the prohibition cannot be avoided by 
having the interested member abstain; the entire governing body is precluded from entering into the 
contract. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 649-650; Stigall, supra, at 569.) 
This requirement has been applied differently in regard to advisory boards or committees. 
For an advisory board, which may have i_nfluence over the contracting decisions, but does not 
execute the contract, the entire board is not disqualified so long as the interested member abstains 
from any type of participation, including giving advice, related to the decision. (82 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 126 (1999).) · 
Such abstention is not possible, however, where an advisory board is statutorily required to 
contain financially interested persons and the Attorney General has found that the conflict of 
interest is deemed allowable by the Legislature. In the 1999 opinion noted above, the Attorney 
General stated that applying the general prohibition of Section 1090 in such circumstances or to 
require the financially interested board members to abstain would undermine the Legislature's  
express determination that interested persons render advice, as members of the advisory board. (82 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 126, supra, at 130.) 
In 2016, we applied this exception where a state grant program required the county to form 
an advisory multidisciplinary team, and an advisory steering committee to recommended services 
for the grant funds. (Page Advice Letter, No. A-16-037.) The enabling statute required the county 
"multidisciplinary team" to include persons trained in child abuse or neglect issues, and qualified to 
provide a broad range of related services. The county was required to document its collaboration 
with partner agencies and children-focused organizations. The county steering committee's make-
up followed the state's suggested template, and included representatives of agencies and 
organizations with financial interests related to these types of children's services. The advice 
focused on the state's purpose in including financially interested members on the advisory bodies, 
and stated: 
It is apparent that these agencies, individuals and private organizations all play 
important roles and provide essential services for CSEC [Commercially Exploited 
Children]. This is the very reason they were singled out for membership on the 
[multidisciplinary team]. In many cases their roles and services are unique. For 
example, only the Juvenile Court, (which is one of the listed agencies), 
adjudicates matters relating to CSEC. To deny funds to the Juvenile Court or 
other agencies or private entities identified in the MOU would be inconsistent 
with the Legislature's intent in funding these types of activities. We do not 
believe that the Legislature contemplated such a result. Accordingly, _SSA [ county 
social services agency] is not prohibited from entering into Proposed Contracts 
with the agencies and private.entities that are represented on the MDT. 
And addressing the steering committee: 
As is the case with the [multidisciplinary team], the advisory role of the Steering 
Committee includes making recommendations for needed services. The MOU 
expressly lists agencies and organizations that may serve on the Steering 
Committee. These agencies and organizations all play important roles and provide 
essential services for CSEC. This is the very reason they were singled out for 
membership on the [multidisciplinary team]. We believe that by including a 
requirement for a steering committee in the Template and approving the MOU 
that contains the requirement, the state contemplated that agencies and 
organizations serving on the Steering Committee because of their expertise would 
be permitted to enter into the subject contracts. Otherwise, the Legislature's 
purpose in adopting a program for funding the needs of CSEC might be thwarted. 
Therefore, we find that under the exception, employers of the individuals serving 
on the Steering Committee may enter into the contracts with SSA to provide 
CSEC services. 
In this matter, the State's JAG Program and the BSCC's RFP requires that that the Local 
Steering Committee be comprised of "a significant cross-section of juvenile and/or criminal justice 
stakeholders within.the applicant county," and tasked with identifying priorities and intervention 
types for the grant. As noted in the language from the RFP, the state specifically identifies the  
traditional stakeholders ( e.g., law enforcement, district attorney) and the nontraditional stakeholders 
( e.g., community and faith-based organizations and social service providers) as the types of 
representation needed on the steering committee. (See fn.5) 
Recognizing the potential for conflict at the state executive steering committee level, the 
BSCC specifically excludes anyone who participated on the JAG Executive Steering Committee 
from receiving funds from the grant applicants who are awarded funds. (See fn. 7.) Notably, the 
BSCC does not state that the conflict of interest laws prohibits the local steering committee 
members from receiving grant funds. As stated in the .2016 advice letter, it is difficult to see how a 
local steering committee required to include local stakeholders could be formed absent potentially 
financially interested members. Therefore, we advise that the narrow exception articulated in 82 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 126 (1999) for advisory boards where the make-up of members is required by 
the program to include financially interested persons, is appli.cable to this matter. The County is not 
prohibited under Section 1090 from entering into contracts with the employer or business of a Local 
JAG Steering Committee member.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Under the Act or Section 1090, may the County enter contracts with the employers of 
individuals who serve on the Local Justice Assistance Grant Program ("JAG") Steering Committee 
using JAG grant funds, where the Local Steering Committee's role is advisory, and the State 
requires the Committee represent a significant cross-section of juvenile and/or criminal justice 
stakeholders?

CONCLUSION: Yes. To the extent that the Local JAG Steering Committee does not meet the criteria for 
having decision-making authority under Regulation 18700( c )(2), individuals who serve on the 
committee are not public officials under the Act and are not prohibited from seeking a grant 
awarded by the County. Where the State requires that the advisory body, the Local Steering 
Committee, be comprised of stakeholders who may have financial interests in the award of grant 
funds, the narrow exception to Section 1090 articulated by the Attorney General, and discussed 
below, is applicable. (82 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 126.) 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTOR
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 Lindsay Beavers Deputy County Counsel Office of the Fresno County Counsel 2220 Tulare Street, Suite 500 Fresno, CA 93 721 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-19-028 Dear Ms. Beavers: August 12, 2019 This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Fresno County regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") and Government Code Section 1090, et seq. 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under these provisions, and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest, including Public Contract Code. · Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (197 5) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assm:nes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Fresno County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We received a written response from the District Attorney stating that office has an interest in the subject of this request, and thus refrains from comment. We did not receive a written response from the Attorney General's Office. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1 ( c )( 5).) QUESTION Under the Act or Section 1090, may the County enter contracts with the employers of individuals who serve on the Local Justice Assistance Grant Program ("JAG") Steering Committee using JAG grant funds, where the Local Steering Committee's role is advisory, and the State requires the Committee represent a significant cross-section of juvenile and/or criminal justice stakeholders? 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. CONCLUSION File No. A-19-028 Page No. 2 Yes. To the extent that the Local JAG Steering Committee does not meet the criteria for having decision-making authority under Regulation 18700( c )(2), individuals who serve on the committee are not public officials under the Act and are not prohibited from seeking a grant awarded by the County. Where the State requires that the advisory body, the Local Steering Committee, be comprised of stakeholders who may have financial interests
